inflation.text: Inflation is roughly on target with expectations re-anchoring, even as recent monthly momentum has turned against the disinflationary trend. Headline CPI was 2.3% y/y in March; core measures sit in a tight band of 2.0% to 2.6%; breadth points to broad-based softening. Expectations are cooling on the business sideâ€”the share of firms expecting inflation above 3% has fallen to 11% (from 50%+ in late 2023)â€”and the consumer 5-year-ahead reading is down 0.4pp over the past year to 3.0%. The complication is March's +0.48% m/m print, well above the pace consistent with 2% annualized; the 3-month annualized rate is at 2.9%.

policy.text: The BoC has held rates at 2.25% for four meetings after a 275bp cutting cycle, and bond markets are starting to price the next move as more likely a hike than another cut. At 2.25%, the policy rate is right at the bottom of the BoC's 2.25-3.25% neutral range and 138 bps below where the Fed sitsâ€”an unusually wide gap, since the BoC cut more aggressively after both central banks peaked in July 2023. Canadian 2-year yields have risen over the past quarter and now trade 66 bps above the policy rate. The BoC finished QT in January 2025; total assets stand at $229B, and banks keep $71B in cash at the BoC overnightâ€”slightly above the $20-60B steady-state target.

gdp.text: Canadian GDP contracted 0.6% Q/Q at an annualized rate in the latest quarter, but the composition is far more constructive than the headline—final domestic demand contributed +2.3pp and net trade +1.5pp, with the entire drag from a -4.2pp inventory swing that typically reverses. Monthly GDP is still positive at +1.0% Y/Y as of February 2026, just below BoC's central potential estimate (1.2-1.3% per the April 2026 MPR). The four-quarter average Q/Q AR of +0.7% remains below potential, and the BoC's most recent output gap of -1.5% to -0.5% signals the economy is running below capacity even as monthly growth holds positive.

labour.text: The Canadian labour market continues to loosen, and the cleaner wage measures now look consistent with the 2% inflation target. Unemployment sits at 6.90%—up 40 bps over three months and at the 84th percentile of its 5-year range—and the V/U ratio at 0.33 runs well below the 0.4–0.6 pre-pandemic baseline, a clear slack signal. Employment has fallen 30 bps over three months while participation held flat, consistent with layoffs absorbing the adjustment rather than workers leaving the labour force. Wage dispersion remains wide—raw LFS at 3.93% against composition-adjusted LFS-Micro at 2.60%—but unit labour costs at 1.56% imply productivity near 1%, and LFS-Micro now runs essentially even with services CPI rather than over a percentage point above.

housing.text: Canadian housing activity is holding near trend with prices still slipping: starts ran 236k SAAR in March and 241k on a 3-month average, up 10% from a year ago but well below CMHC's affordability-restoring pace of 430–500k. Prices are falling on both sides of the market—CREA MLS HPI -4.8% Y/Y and NHPI -2.3%—with resale leading new-build, the typical pattern as builders are slow to cut asking prices. The mortgage-payment-to-income ratio has eased to 0.427, down 8.2% Y/Y and back to the 37th percentile of its 5-year range, signaling that lower rates are passing through to affordability. Permits are 2.8% below year-ago levels though, so the leading-indicator pipeline is softening even as current starts hold up.

financial.text: Oil is doing all the inflation work—WTI is up 74% over the past year to $105/bbl, mechanically adding about 2.7pp to headline CPI through gasoline. CAD provides essentially no offset; USDCAD at 1.3575 is roughly 2% firmer over the year, and daily-change correlation with WTI sits at just +0.10—the petrocurrency link looks decoupled. The dollar's firmness likely reflects non-commodity drivers—rate differentials and FX risk premium per BoC's recent decompositions—rather than energy; the framework's read is partial without CEER or an explicit FX risk-premium series. WCS sits at a $9 discount to WTI, back within the post-TMX range after last month's unusual premium.
financial.review_flags:
- **"WCS sits at a $9 discount to WTI"** — The data shows `WCS-to-WTI discount: $-8.89/bbl`, i.e. a negative discount, meaning WCS is at roughly a $9 *premium* to WTI, not a discount. The blurb has the sign backwards.
- **"back within the post-TMX range after last month's unusual premium"** — The post-TMX range per the framework is a positive discount of <$12. A ~$9 *premium* (the current reading) is outside that range, not back within it. The blurb also asserts "last month's" premium as the unusual episode when the data point in hand (March 2026) is itself the premium reading; nothing in the supplied data supports a separate prior-month premium that has now resolved.
